definition: Caching strategy where the application checks the cache first and, on a miss, loads from the datastore and populates the cache itself. Also called lazy loading.
Loading data only when it is first needed (e.g. populating a cache on a miss) rather than ahead of time.
Cache write policy where each write goes to the cache and the datastore together, keeping them consistent at the cost of write latency.
Caching where the cache itself loads missing data from the datastore transparently, so the application only ever talks to the cache.
Cache write policy (also write-behind) where writes hit only the cache and are flushed to the datastore asynchronously later — fast, but risks data loss if the cache fails before flushing.
Another name for write-back caching: writes are acknowledged by the cache and persisted to the datastore asynchronously.
Time To Live: how long a cached entry stays valid before it expires and must be refreshed.
Content Delivery Network: caches copies of content on edge servers near users to cut latency and offload the origin.
In a distributed system, during a network partition you must choose between Consistency and Availability — you cannot have both.
Consistency model where replicas may briefly diverge but converge to the same value once writes stop propagating.
Every read reflects the most recent committed write, as if there were a single up-to-date copy.
The strongest single-object consistency: operations appear to take effect instantly in one global order consistent with real time.
A session guarantee that a client always sees its own prior writes on subsequent reads, even if other clients lag.
Maps keys and nodes onto the same ring so adding/removing a node only moves about K/N keys, instead of remapping nearly everything as plain modulo hashing does.
Splitting a dataset across multiple nodes (partitions) so each holds only a subset, to scale beyond one machine.
Dividing data into independent subsets (partitions/shards) that can live on different nodes.
A node or shard that receives a disproportionate share of data or traffic, becoming a bottleneck.
Keeping copies of data on multiple nodes for availability and read scaling.
A minimum number of replicas that must respond. With N replicas, requiring R + W > N ensures reads and writes overlap so a read sees the latest write.
A buffer between producer and consumer that decouples them, absorbs bursts, and lets each scale and fail independently.
A property where performing an operation multiple times has the same effect as performing it once — essential when messages can be delivered more than once.
Safe to repeat: doing the operation again produces the same result as doing it once.
Delivery guarantee where a message is delivered one or more times, so duplicates are possible and handlers must be idempotent.
Delivery semantics where each message takes effect exactly one time — usually achieved via deduplication/idempotency on top of at-least-once.
Component that distributes incoming requests across multiple backend instances and routes around unhealthy ones.
Load-balancing algorithm that sends each new request to the next server in rotation.
Load-balancing algorithm that routes each request to the server with the fewest in-flight connections, adapting to uneven request durations.
Capping how many requests a client may make in a time window to protect a service from overload or abuse.
Rate-limiting algorithm: tokens refill at a steady rate into a bucket of fixed capacity; each request spends a token, allowing bursts up to the bucket size.
Signaling upstream producers to slow down when a downstream consumer cannot keep up, preventing overload.
Balanced tree structure used by database indexes to turn a full-table scan (O(n)) into a sorted O(log n) lookup.
An auxiliary structure (often a B-tree) that speeds up reads on a column at the cost of extra storage and slower writes.
A space-efficient probabilistic structure that answers 'definitely not present' or 'possibly present' — used to skip expensive lookups.
An architecture of small, independently deployable services that communicate over the network.
A single deployable application containing all functionality, with in-process calls and one codebase.
Event-driven coordination where each service reacts to events independently, with no central controller.
Coordination where a central component explicitly drives each step of a workflow.
Command Query Responsibility Segregation: use separate models (often separate stores) for writes (commands) and reads (queries).
Persisting state as an ordered log of immutable events; current state is derived by replaying them.
Ports & adapters architecture: the domain defines interfaces (ports) that infrastructure adapters implement, keeping the core free of infrastructure.
Another name for hexagonal architecture: domain-owned ports (interfaces) plugged into by adapters (DB, HTTP, etc.).
A sequence of local transactions across services, each with a compensating action, used to keep data consistent without a distributed transaction.
An action that semantically undoes a previously committed step of a saga when a later step fails (e.g. refunding a charge).
A distributed-transaction protocol (2PC) that atomically commits across nodes via a prepare phase then a commit phase, at the cost of blocking and a coordinator.
A single L7 entry point in front of services that centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, routing) and can aggregate calls.
Resilience pattern that, after repeated failures to a dependency, 'opens' to fail fast, then trials recovery (half-open) before closing again.
Transactional outbox pattern: write an event to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the business change, then relay it — solving the dual-write problem.
Incremental migration pattern: route through a facade and replace a legacy system piece by piece until it can be retired, avoiding a big-bang rewrite.
Model Context Protocol: an open protocol that standardizes how LLM applications connect to external tools, resources, and prompts.
A lightweight remote-procedure-call protocol using JSON messages; MCP exchanges JSON-RPC 2.0 requests, responses, and notifications.
Standard input/output transport: the host launches a local MCP server as a subprocess and exchanges messages over its stdin/stdout.
MCP's HTTP-based transport for remote servers, supporting server-sent streaming — as opposed to stdio for local subprocess servers.
MCP feature where a server requests an LLM completion from the host (server-initiated), with the host expected to keep a human in the loop.
MCP capability where the client tells the server which URI/filesystem boundaries it should operate within, scoping its access.
